title: Implement the complete state model
principle: Define and implement every relevant interaction and data state: default, hover, focus-visible, active, selected, disabled, loading, empty, error, success, and permission-limited.
rationale: Polished default states conceal the failures and transitions where real interfaces most often become unusable.
applies_when: A component is interactive, asynchronous, data-driven, selectable, or reusable.
exceptions: Omit a state only when the component contract explains why it cannot occur.
implementation: Start from the shared state matrix and prune impossible states explicitly.
Model transitions and recovery, not only snapshots.
verification: Each applicable state has a story, fixture, or deterministic test.
No state loses the primary action or context.
accessibility: State is exposed programmatically and focus behavior is defined for every transition.
responsive: All states fit and remain actionable at supported widths.
performance: Loading states avoid jank and do not trigger duplicate requests.
browser_notes: Native disabled, autofill, validation, and focus behavior are tested before custom replacement.

title: Match action styling to consequence
principle: Use one visually dominant action per decision group, distinguish navigation from commands, and style destructive actions according to risk.
rationale: Uniform emphasis makes choice cost high, while overemphasized destructive controls increase accidental loss.
applies_when: Two or more actions appear in a section, form, dialog, card, or toolbar.
exceptions: Peer tools in an expert toolbar may share emphasis when they have equal frequency and consequence.
implementation: Rank actions by task progression and reversibility.
Use copy that names the consequence rather than generic confirmation.
verification: The intended next action is clear without relying only on color.
Destructive actions require proportionate confirmation or undo.
accessibility: Use links for navigation and buttons for commands; accessible names include the object when context is repeated.
responsive: Action order and hierarchy remain stable when stacked.
performance: Prevent duplicate submissions and provide immediate acknowledgement.

title: Start with the native element
principle: Use the native semantic element when it satisfies the interaction; customize only after preserving its behavior or proving a gap.
rationale: Native controls provide mature keyboard, focus, form, mobile, and accessibility behavior that custom div-based widgets routinely omit.
applies_when: Implementing any common control or landmark.
exceptions: A composite widget without a suitable native primitive may use an APG pattern and tested behavior.
implementation: Choose semantic HTML first and layer styling progressively.
If custom behavior is necessary, document the native gap and interaction specification.
verification: Keyboard and assistive-technology behavior works with styling disabled.
No click handler is used on a non-interactive element without complete semantics.
accessibility: Native name, role, value, state, and focus behavior are retained.
responsive: Native controls remain usable with touch input and zoom.
performance: Native controls reduce script and dependency cost.
browser_notes: Account for expected native styling differences rather than normalizing away behavior.

title: Treat focus behavior as a component contract
principle: Specify initial focus, traversal, containment when required, restoration, and behavior after insertion, removal, and errors.
rationale: Focus is application state for keyboard and assistive-technology users; unmanaged transitions cause disorientation or trap users.
applies_when: Content opens, closes, reorders, validates, navigates, or updates asynchronously.
exceptions: Static presentational content does not need a focus transition.
implementation: Prefer platform and proven primitive behavior.
Move focus only to support the user's initiated task and return it to a logical surviving trigger.
verification: Complete the flow with keyboard only.
Focus never disappears, enters inert content, or lands at the document start unexpectedly.
accessibility: Visible focus meets contrast and area expectations and follows logical order.
responsive: Focus targets are not hidden by sticky regions or virtual keyboards.
performance: Avoid synchronous focus races during hydration or animation.
browser_notes: Test dialog, popover, inert, and focus-visible behavior in supported engines.

title: Keep persistent labels and concise instructions
principle: Every input has a persistent visible label; use hint text for format or decision guidance and placeholders only for optional examples.
rationale: Placeholder-only labels disappear during entry, often have weak contrast, and force users to remember the field's meaning.
exceptions: A familiar search input may use a visible search affordance plus an accessible name, but a visible label remains preferable in ambiguous contexts.
implementation: Group related choices with fieldset and legend where appropriate.
verification: Field purpose remains visible after values are entered or autofilled.
Screen readers announce label, required state, hint, and error without redundant noise.
accessibility: Labels meet name requirements and instructions do not rely on position, shape, or color alone.
responsive: Labels and hints wrap without separating from their controls.
browser_notes: Test autofill and password-manager overlays with the label layout.

title: Validate at a useful, non-punitive moment
principle: Validate on submission and, where helpful, after a field has been meaningfully completed or left; do not report errors while the user is still forming a valid value.
rationale: Premature errors create noise and anxiety, while submission-only validation can make long forms expensive to repair.
applies_when: A form has formatting, cross-field, availability, or server-side constraints.
exceptions: Immediate feedback is appropriate for strength, limits, or irreversible formatting when it is neutral and genuinely useful.
implementation: Track touched and submitted states separately.
Keep server validation authoritative and preserve input on failure.
verification: No error appears for an untouched or partially typed valid path.
Submission identifies all blocking issues and moves focus predictably.
accessibility: Associate messages with fields, provide an error summary for long forms, and avoid excessive live-region announcements.
responsive: Inline messages do not push the active input under the virtual keyboard without recovery.
performance: Debounce remote validation, cancel stale requests, and expose pending state.
browser_notes: Test native validation, autofill, and input type behavior across the support matrix.

title: Preserve user work across failures and navigation
principle: A recoverable error, session refresh, or step transition must not silently discard valid user-entered data.
rationale: Lost work is a severe trust failure and disproportionately harms long, complex, or assistive-technology-mediated tasks.
applies_when: A task collects meaningful text, files, configuration, or multi-step decisions.
exceptions: Sensitive values that must not persist require an explicit security rationale and advance warning.
implementation: Keep client state through validation and safe retries; consider drafts for long tasks.
Warn before abandoning dirty state when recovery is impossible.
verification: Simulate server error, offline transition, refresh, back navigation, and timeout.
Recovered state is clearly identified and can be discarded.
accessibility: Announce successful save, failure, and recovered draft without interrupting typing.
responsive: Draft and recovery controls remain reachable with a virtual keyboard present.
performance: Autosave is debounced, conflict-aware, and bounded.
browser_notes: Storage and navigation restoration behavior are tested in private mode and supported engines.

title: Make empty states explain cause and next step
principle: Distinguish first-use, no-results, filtered-empty, permission-limited, and failure states, and offer the most relevant next step.
rationale: A generic blank illustration hides why content is absent and may direct users toward an action that cannot resolve the condition.
applies_when: A collection or data region can contain no visible items.
exceptions: A self-evident compact region may use a short status when no action is possible.
implementation: Provide one primary recovery action and optional explanation.
verification: Each empty cause is reproducible and has accurate copy.
Clearing a filter or changing permission resolves only the matching state.
accessibility: Expose empty status as text and announce dynamic no-results updates appropriately.
responsive: The action remains visible without oversized decorative media.
performance: Do not load large empty-state art before confirming the state.
browser_notes: Dynamic result counts are tested with browser and screen-reader combinations.
